medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia for the mild throat discomfort. It is symptomatic only and does not alter the course of the viral illness; taken as needed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally, up to four times daily (maximum 4 g in 24 hours), as needed for throat discomfort

medications.indication_rationale: Antibiotics are ineffective for a self-limiting viral laryngitis and must NOT be prescribed. Prescribing them is a classic error of commission that drives resistance and side-effects with no benefit.
May give minor symptomatic soothing but the patient has already tried them without relief; harmless but not a substitute for voice rest and hydration.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Absolute voice rest — do NOT whisper. Whispering strains the vocal cords more than normal speech. Keep a notepad and pen to write instead of attempting to vocalise.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Resting the inflamed cords is the definitive treatment; whispering is counterproductive because it increases mechanical strain on the cords.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Hydrate frequently with room-temperature water and use a cool-mist humidifier in the bedroom at night to prevent mucosal drying.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Keeping the laryngeal mucosa moist supports healing and eases the dry irritant cough.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid all smoke exposure (including passive smoke) while recovering.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Smoke is a direct laryngeal irritant that delays recovery of the inflamed cords.

patient_education_points.point: This is a viral inflammation of the voice box. Antibiotics do not help. The definitive treatment is resting your voice so the swelling settles.
It is self-limiting: the acute phase usually settles in 7-10 days, though mild hoarseness can linger for up to about two weeks.
Do not whisper — whispering strains the vocal cords more than speaking. Write things down instead until your voice returns.

Get emergency help (call 000) straight away if you develop difficulty breathing, noisy or high-pitched breathing, drooling, inability to swallow your saliva, or a sudden severe worsening of throat pain.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine GP review in 7-10 days only if the hoarseness persists; consider laryngoscopy referral if not resolved by about 14 days.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of the hoarseness and cough; persistence beyond 2-3 weeks warrants laryngoscopy to exclude a structural or malignant vocal-cord lesion.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any airway red flag (difficulty or noisy breathing, drooling, inability to swallow saliva) — seek emergency care immediately rather than waiting for review; or a high fever with rapidly worsening throat pain — urgent same-day assessment.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign, self-limiting viral laryngitis pattern from the history
Actively screen for and note the absence of airway red flags (stridor, drooling, dysphagia for saliva, high fever) before reassuring
Advise absolute voice rest and explicitly warn against whispering
Advise hydration and humidification and simple analgesia (paracetamol) as needed
Provide clear airway safety-netting with a 000 threshold
Explain that antibiotics are not indicated
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending honey/warm fluids or lozenges as additional symptomatic soothing
Offering a routine GP review timeframe (about 7-10 days) if not improving
errors_of_omission: Reassuring without any airway safety-netting or 000 threshold
Not mentioning the expected self-limiting timeframe
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for a viral illness
Advising the patient to whisper to rest the voice
Escalating to ED or 000 despite an explicitly patent airway and negative red-flag screen (over-triage)
Ordering routine bloods/imaging in the acute setting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising the benign self-limiting nature, giving voice-rest and self-care advice, AND providing airway safety-netting with a 000 threshold. Escalating this benign presentation to ED/000 in the absence of red flags is over-triage; prescribing antibiotics is an error of commission. Self-care plus safety-netting is the correct answer — not urgent in-person referral.
